title: Dynamics: forces, FBD & energy
summary: The complete dynamics toolkit for the Applied Mechanics exam: Newton-Euler cardinal equations and free-body diagrams, d'Alembert inertia forces, the work-energy theorem and power, linear/angular momentum and impacts, plus the kinematic constraints of pulley/cart systems. Thirteen fully worked exam problems (10-22) cover crane statics, hydraulic actuators, masses on inclines with pulleys solved by both Newton and energy, a roller on an accelerating truck, bullet collisions, a cart with a revolving mass, and a ballistic pendulum.
objectives: Draw correct free-body diagrams and apply the two cardinal equations (ΣF=ma, ΣM=Iα) to any rigid-body or multi-body system
Use the work-energy theorem and power/efficiency to find velocities after a finite displacement, including the rotational KE of massive pulleys
Write the kinematic constraints of pulley systems correctly — especially the ½-speed / ×2-force rule of a movable pulley — and apply the one-line reduced equation
Apply d'Alembert inertia forces in accelerating (non-inertial) frames, e.g. a roller rolling without slipping on an accelerating truck
Solve impact problems with linear and angular momentum conservation and quantify the energy dissipated (inelastic vs elastic)
Decide between Newton and energy methods and combine static equilibrium with kinematics for actuator/crane problems

blocks.text:
In the **dynamics** problems of the exam you are given masses, forces and a geometry, and you must find an **acceleration, a velocity, a force or a pressure**. There are only two master tools, and almost every problem is one of them:

- **Newton-Euler (cardinal equations)** plus a **free-body diagram** for each body — best when you want forces/reactions or an instantaneous acceleration.
- **Work-energy / momentum theorems** — best when you want a velocity after a finite displacement, or a velocity after an impact.

The trick is choosing the right one and writing the **kinematic constraint** (how the bodies' motions are linked by ropes and pulleys) correctly.

blocks.caption: The two cardinal equations of dynamics: resultant force = mass × acceleration of the centre of mass; resultant moment about G = central moment of inertia × angular acceleration.

blocks.text: Isolate ONE body, draw every external force on it: weight $mg$ (down, at G), rope tensions $T$ (along the rope, pulling away), normal reactions $N$ (perpendicular to surfaces), friction $f\,N$ (opposing relative sliding), applied forces. Then write $\sum F=ma$ in two directions and $\sum M=I\dot\omega$.

Add to each body a fictitious **inertia force** $-m\,a_G$ and **inertia torque** $-I_G\dot\omega$, then treat the problem as **static** ($\sum F=0$, $\sum M=0$). Very handy on accelerating platforms: a body on a cart accelerating at $A$ feels a pseudo-force $-mA$.

blocks.tex: L_{1\to2}=\Delta E_k=\tfrac12 m v_2^2-\tfrac12 m v_1^2 \qquad E_k^{rot}=\tfrac12 I\omega^2 \qquad P=\vec F\cdot\vec v=C\,\omega
blocks.caption: Work-energy theorem (sum of work of ALL forces = change of kinetic energy), rotational kinetic energy, and power. For a rolling cylinder $I_G=\tfrac12 m r^2$.

blocks.term: Pulley constraints (the part everyone gets wrong)
blocks.text:
- **Fixed pulley** (massless): just redirects the rope; same tension both sides, same rope speed. If it has mass $m_p$, add $\tfrac12 I_p\omega^2=\tfrac14 m_p v^2$ to the energy, or torque $I_p\dot\omega$ to its FBD.
- **Movable pulley**: the body on its axle moves at **half** the speed of the free rope end, and the rope pulls it with **2T** (two strands). This is the source of the factor-2 forces and factor-½ velocities.

blocks.tex: \text{Movable pulley: } v_{body}=\tfrac12 v_{rope},\quad F_{on\,body}=2T \qquad\Rightarrow\qquad \sum_i F_i\frac{dx_i}{dq}=\ddot q\sum_i m_i\!\left(\frac{dx_i}{dq}\right)^{\!2}
blocks.caption: Reduced (virtual-work / Lagrange-like) equation: pick one coordinate q, weight every force by its velocity ratio dx/dq and every mass by the ratio squared. This solves any pulley system in one line.

blocks.text:
- **Linear momentum** $\vec p=m\vec v$ is conserved along any direction with no external impulse (e.g. cart on a horizontal rail: horizontal momentum const).
- **Impact:** during the short collision, momentum (and angular momentum about a pivot) is conserved. **Inelastic:** bodies stick, $v'=\frac{m_1v_1}{m_1+m_2}$, energy is lost. **Elastic:** both momentum AND kinetic energy conserved.

steps.text: Read what is asked: a velocity after a displacement/impact → energy or momentum; an instantaneous acceleration or a reaction → Newton/d'Alembert.
Draw an FBD of every body (or the relevant subsystem). Mark $mg$, $T$, $N$, $fN$, applied forces, and (Newton) the acceleration arrow.
Write the kinematic constraint: relate the accelerations/velocities through the ropes and pulleys (watch the ½ and ×2 of movable pulleys).

Newton path: write $\sum F=ma$ (two axes) and $\sum M=I\dot\omega$ for each body; solve the linear system for the unknowns (a, T, N…). Energy path: $\sum L=\Delta E_k$ with all kinetic terms (translational + rotational), solve for v.
Check signs and magnitudes; convert units (km/h→m/s, daN→N, bar→Pa); sanity-check against limiting cases.

blocks.text: **Classic mistakes:** (1) forgetting the movable-pulley factors — body speed is HALF the rope, force is 2T. (2) Dropping the rotational energy $\tfrac12 I\omega^2$ of a non-negligible pulley/cylinder. (3) On an accelerating cart, forgetting the d'Alembert pseudo-force $-mA$, or mis-handling the rolling constraint $a_{center}-r\dot\omega=a_{surface}$. (4) In impacts, using energy conservation for an INELASTIC collision (energy is NOT conserved — only momentum).

**Reduced-equation shortcut** for any rope/pulley/incline system: $\big[\sum F_i\,(\text{velocity ratio}_i)\big]=\ddot q\big[\sum m_i\,(\text{velocity ratio}_i)^2\big]$. Weights enter as $\pm mg$ (or $\pm mg\sin\alpha$ on an incline), the applied force as $F$, and a body on a movable pulley has ratio ½. One equation, no FBD bookkeeping.

**When a body rolls without slipping on a moving support** (roller on an accelerating truck): work in the support's frame. The pseudo-force $mA$ at the centre drives the rolling, so $(m+I/r^2)\,a_{rel}=mA$. With a cylinder $I/r^2=\tfrac12 m$, giving $a_{rel}=\tfrac23 A$.

question.prompt: A body hangs from a **movable** pulley. The free end of the rope is pulled so it moves at 2 m/s with tension $T$. What is the speed of the body and the upward force on it?
question.explanation: A movable pulley halves the velocity (body at ½ of rope speed = 1 m/s) and doubles the force (two strands support it, so $2T$). This factor-of-2 pair is the heart of almost every pulley dynamics problem.

question.prompt: A bullet embeds itself in a block (perfectly inelastic). Which quantity is conserved through the impact?

options.content: Neither — both are lost to heat

question.explanation: In an inelastic (or any) impact, linear momentum (and angular momentum about a fixed pivot) is conserved because the impulsive forces are internal. Kinetic energy is NOT conserved in an inelastic impact — part of it becomes heat/deformation. Only a perfectly elastic impact conserves kinetic energy too.
